results.possibleIdentity: Ralph Martel is a likely intended identity. He was an engineer who collaborated with artist Jean Dupuy on the interactive work "Heart Beats Dust" (1968), which won first prize in a competition sponsored by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the American Foundation on Automation and Employment. The work, which featured a glass cube containing red dust (Lithol Rubine BK) activated by the amplified rhythm of a human heartbeat, was shown at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in the landmark 1968 exhibition "The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age" and at the Brooklyn Museum's E.A.T. exhibition "Some More Beginnings." Martel was born in 1935 and was highly active in the late 1960s and early 1970s within the New York art and technology scene.

Ralph Merrill Evans (1908–1974) was a prominent American color scientist and researcher who worked for the Eastman Kodak Company in Rochester, New York. As the superintendent of the Color Control Department, he was a leading figure in the development of color photography technology and color perception. He authored several influential books, including "An Introduction to Color" (1948) and "The Perception of Color" (1974). His work on the intersection of visual science, technology, and image-making made him a pioneer in the fields of color theory and optics, which were areas of significant interest for collaborators within Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and early 1970s.

W. Ralph Merrill was a modernist architect based in Dallas, Texas, active from the late 1930s through at least the 1970s. He is recognized for his contributions to mid-century modern residential architecture, including the design of homes in Dallas's Turtle Creek Park and Preston Hollow neighborhoods. Merrill was also an accomplished artist and illustrator; his detailed pencil drawings were featured in publications such as "Hawaii: A Literary Chronicle" (1970) and various architectural journals like "Pencil Points" (later Progressive Architecture). He was a member of the American Institute of Architects (AIA) and the National Society of Interior Designers (NSID).

results.possibleIdentity: Cliff Glynn was a New York-based graphic designer and corporate identity specialist active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He specialized in promotional materials and corporate graphics, and by 1958, he was serving as the First Vice President of the Artists Guild of New York. His professional work included a project or firm titled 'Cliff Glynn corporate graphics,' which appeared in the 1974 Catalog of Copyright Entries. Given his professional prominence in the New York design community and his specialization in corporate promotion during the height of the organization's activity, he is likely associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which frequently collaborated with designers to produce exhibition materials, technical documentation, and organizational graphics.
